candidates.title: Hyung Won Chung
candidates.textPreview: Hyung Won Chung I am a Research Scientist at OpenAI. At OpenAI, my research focuses on reasoning and agents. I was a Foundational Contributor of o1-preview (Sep. 2024), o1 (Dec. 2024), Deep Research (Feb. 2025). I currently focus on coding agents and led the Codex mini model-training. Prior to OpenAI, I was at Google Brain where I worked on whatever bottleneck stood in the way of further scaling: building JAX-based large-scale training system (T5X), training large models (e.g. PaLM), instruction fine-tuning (Flan-PaLM and Flan-T5 model families) and reasoning. Prior to Google Brain, I did my PhD at MIT. I am originally from South Korea (Korean name: 정형원) and currently live in Mountain View,...

candidates.textPreview: Post Log inSign up Post user avatar Hyung Won Chung @hwchung27 I often hear people say they believe the world will be vastly different in 5 years. Yet, many of them plan life and career as if things won’t change much. Why such discrepancy? Humans have bias towards downplaying major future changes, especially the fast changing ones. Perhaps this reflects a subconscious desire for lower uncertainty. I find it helpful simply to acknowledge such biases exist. Over time, I collect my experiences (both mine and friends’) where we underestimated the future changes and do post-mortems to learn from those. candidates.textPreview: Post Log inSign up Post user avatar Hyung Won Chung @hwchung27 I often hear people say they believe the world will be vastly different in 5 years. Yet, many of them plan life and career as if things won’t change much. Why such discrepancy? Humans have bias towards downplaying major future changes, especially the fast changing ones. Perhaps this reflects a subconscious desire for lower uncertainty. I find it helpful simply to acknowledge such biases exist. Over time, I collect my experiences (both mine and friends’) where we underestimated the future changes and do post-mortems to learn from those. candidates.textPreview: Musings on the Alignment Problem Should we control AI instead of aligning it? (Spoiler: no) Jan Leike Jan 24, 2025 The basic pitch for AI control is to build systems around capable models that ensure they can’t cause harm even if they were misaligned. For example, a misaligned model could deliberately introduce subtle mistakes or bugs or try to go rogue and self-exfiltrate. Control techniques aim to catch this kind of behavior and mitigate it. There are two reasons to do this: 1. So we can use or deploy models that aren’t sufficiently aligned 2. candidates.textPreview: Gemini 2.0 and the evolution of agentic AI | Oriol Vinyals Google DeepMind 886000 subscribers 2177 likes Description 98261 views Posted: 12 Dec 2024 In this episode, Hannah is joined by Oriol Vinyals, VP of Drastic Research and Gemini co-lead. They discuss the evolution of agents from single-task models to more general-purpose models capable of broader applications, like Gemini. Vinyals guides Hannah through the two-step process behind multi modal models: pre-training (imitation learning) and post-training (reinforcement learning).
